narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, your background brings unique strengths and challenges that the admissions model carefully considered alongside your academic record. The model predicts an 84% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—law schools use this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. This high failure risk stems primarily from three key factors in your profile that had the strongest sway in the model's decision. The most influential was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is much lower than the typical student's 5.5th decile; this signaled to the model a significant dip in performance that strongly pushed toward expecting bar exam struggles. Next, your LSAT score of 20, well below the average of about 37, further reinforced this risk by indicating foundational skills the model links closely to bar success. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile of 5th—slightly under the average 5.7th—provided some counterbalance, nudging the prediction a bit toward passing and showing early potential. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 (below the 3.2 average), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers here. Compared to typical students, who often enter with stronger late-stage grades and higher LSATs, your profile stood out as higher-risk despite being full-time like over 90% of applicants. This doesn't define your abilities—models like this weigh patterns from past students, and individual stories often defy them. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later academic performance through targeted prep or additional coursework, and reapplying after building a clearer track record of improvement. Many applicants in similar situations, including those from Hispanic backgrounds, succeed by addressing these exact gaps and gaining practical experience like internships. Your early decile shows promise—lean into that resilience for your next steps.
